name: Sylvani
description:
The Sylvani are not a species in the way that animals understand the word. They are a single, distributed botanical intelligence — a mycelial-neural network that achieved awareness when its root systems grew large enough to sustain recursive self-reference. On their garden world of Verdanthis, the Sylvani network spans every continent, threaded through soil and stone, connecting billions of arboreal nodes into a mind that thinks in seasons and remembers in geological epochs. A single Sylvani 'individual' is more accurately a localised concentration of the network's attention — a grove that the greater mind has chosen to focus through, the way a human might focus on a single finger.

The Sylvani did not choose to expand into the galaxy. They grew into it, the way a forest grows into a clearing — slowly, inevitably, and without anything resembling hurry. Their spacecraft are not built but cultivated: living vessels of engineered heartwood and photosynthetic hull-membrane, grown over decades in orbital nurseries, each one a self-sustaining ecosystem. But beneath this patient expansion lies a deeper urgency that the Sylvani do not discuss with outsiders. They have felt it in the soil of a hundred worlds — a creeping sterility, a wrongness in the mycorrhizal web. Ecosystems that should thrive are quietly failing. Microbial diversity is collapsing on worlds that have never seen industrial contamination. The Sylvani call it the Withering, and they believe it is not natural. Something is poisoning the galaxy from the roots up, and they have spread themselves across the stars to find the source before it reaches Verdanthis.

To animal species, the Sylvani are simultaneously the most approachable and most alien of the elder civilisations. They are patient, generous with knowledge, and genuinely interested in the wellbeing of ecosystems everywhere. They are also profoundly unsettling in their timescales and priorities. A Sylvani ambassador might take three years to respond to a trade proposal — not from rudeness but because the network is still deliberating. They do not experience urgency the way animals do. They do not fear death, because no individual node is the self; the network endures. Their art is expressed through designed ecosystems — forests sculpted over millennia into living cathedrals of breathtaking complexity, where every organism plays a role in a grand biological symphony.

The Sylvani's great internal tension is one of tempo. The Old Growth — the ancient core network on Verdanthis — counsels patience above all else. It has survived ice ages, asteroid impacts, and continental drift. It sees no reason to rush now. The New Growth — younger networks seeded on colony worlds, exposed to the faster rhythms of animal civilisations — has begun to argue that patience is a luxury the Withering will not permit. For the first time in their billion-year history, some parts of the Sylvani are arguing that they need to act fast. The Old Growth finds this almost incomprehensibly distressing. A mind that thinks in millennia is being asked to think in decades, and the strain is beginning to show.
